question: According to the introductory seminar slides, which specific Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) guidance must be combined with "The Code" when addressing clinical issues in practice?
options: NMC Guidance on Professional Boundaries (2019)
NMC Guidance on Raising Concerns (2025)
NMC Guidelines for Revalidation Cycles (2022)
NMC Delegation Standards for Healthcare Assistants (2020)

question: The course slides outline that the NMC guidance on raising concerns should be utilized in combination with specific local and corporate parameters. Which of the following is explicitly listed?
options: Whistleblowing policies issued by your employer
Financial audit templates compiled by ward administrators
The individual nurse's employment contract log
National pharmaceutical manufacturing directories • • • • Professional Practice Evaluation • Exam Paper 4 of 20 Page 1 of 8

question: How is 'Safeguarding' explicitly defined in the core text of your course slides?
options: To optimize the economic output of regional trusts while managing staff resource allocations
To protect an individual’s health, wellbeing and human rights; enabling them to live free from harm, abuse and neglect
To provide legal defense indemnity documentation for senior clinical care management tiers
To coordinate outpatient appointment timelines to meet national target frameworks

question: The lecture slides define 'Abuse' as a violation of an individual's human and civil rights. What three explicit alternative drivers or causes behind abuse are named?
options: Malice, strategic intent, structural bias
Deliberate intent, negligence or ignorance
Cognitive impairment, resource scarcity, physical strain
Environmental failure, systematic pressure, poor oversight

question: According to the legal frameworks listed on Slide 6, which piece of legislation sets out the clear legal framework for how we should protect adults at risk of abuse or neglect?
options: The Mental Capacity Act 2005
The Care Act (2014)
The Human Rights Act 1998
The Equality Act 2010

question: Under the Care Act (2014) statutory criteria, safeguarding adults applies explicitly to any adult who meets which specific set of factors?
options: Is over the age of 65 and resides within a residential care setting independently
Has care and support needs, is experiencing or at risk of abuse/neglect, and is unable to protect themselves because of those needs
Lacks formal cognitive processing capacity as determined by two separate medical consultants
Requires acute mechanical ventilation and is under full public financial funding streams Professional Practice Evaluation • Exam Paper 4 of 20 Page 2 of 8

question: How many distinct core principles are addressed within the framework of The Care Act (2014) in your seminar slides?
options: Four principles
Six principles
Eight principles
Ten principles

question: Which of the Care Act principles is defined exactly as: "patients should be supported and encouraged to make their own decisions"?
options: Protection
Empowerment
Proportionality
Partnership

question: Under The Care Act (2014) framework, how is the principle of 'Prevention' explicitly defined?
options: The response should be the least intrusive and most appropriate to the risk presented
It is better to take action before harm occurs rather than waiting until it does
There should be dedicated support pathways for those in the greatest social need
All clinical safeguarding practice should be fully transparent and audited

question: Which principle dictates that a safeguarding response "should be the least intrusive and most appropriate to the risk"?
options: Accountability
Proportionality
Partnership
Empowerment Professional Practice Evaluation • Exam Paper 4 of 20 Page 3 of 8

question: How many distinct types of abuse are explicitly tabulated in the course text on Slide 8?
options: 7 types
10 types
12 types
15 types

question: Which category of abuse from your curriculum list directly encompasses "acts of omission"?
options: Organisational or institutional abuse
Neglect or acts of omission
Discriminatory abuse
Financial or material abuse

question: The slides describe that incidents of abuse typically come to light through four specific mechanisms. Which mechanism is explicitly listed?
options: Random local pharmacy stock audits
Disclosure, witnessing an incident, observing signs/symptoms, or online platforms
Reviewing patient length-of-stay parameters across the trust dashboard
Standard cross-checking of medical fee codes

question: According to Slide 11, under what explicit circumstance must a clinician immediately call 999?
options: If a patient's relative requests access to paper chart summaries
If the person is in immediate danger or needs urgent medical attention
If a historical financial discrepancy is disclosed by an administrative colleague
If a student nurse requests a change to their clinical placement log Professional Practice Evaluation • Exam Paper 4 of 20 Page 4 of 8

question: If a clinical situation is not urgent but you firmly believe a crime has taken place, what specific action is directed on Slide 11?
options: E-mail the Care Quality Commission via their standard notification log
Phone the police on 101
Wait until the close of the full weekly shift block to file a hardcopy file
Conduct an independent investigation alongside adjacent ward staff

question: In Case Scenario 1, a 72-year-old man is admitted for dementia assessment. His carer reports a history of falls, but your examination reveals what specific anomaly?
options: An acute fracture that requires immediate surgical fixation pinning
Bruises which are not consistent with falling
An unexpected rapid improvement in short term spatial processing scores
Significant dehydration accompanied by severe stage 4 pressure ulcers

question: In Case Scenario 2, Mrs. Jones is given a sedative injection by the nurse in charge every single time she shouts out "to calm her down". This pattern of practice raises major flags for what type of abuse?
options: Financial exploitation
Organisational or institutional abuse (involving potential chemical restraint)
Self-neglect progression
Discriminatory profiling exclusively

question: During the events of Case Scenario 2, what explicit instruction does the nurse in charge give you regarding Mrs. Jones's lunchtime care?
options: To document her food intake parameters within the electronic system immediately
To leave the food on the side and not wake the patient up as she would start shouting again
To transfer her care directly to an auxiliary volunteer or family member
To prepare an additional verbal SBAR briefing before approaching the bed locker Professional Practice Evaluation • Exam Paper 4 of 20 Page 5 of 8

question: In Case Scenario 3, a day-shift colleague explains that they left medications on a table for dementia patients to take independently, stating: "they don't know anyway what they take or when". Why is this an explicit safeguarding and abuse issue?
options: Because medications can only be distributed by pharmacy consultants legally
It represents severe neglect or acts of omission, posing immediate clinical risk to vulnerable adults
Because the patient's family members did not co-sign the tracking receipt sheet
It alters the ward budget parameters for scheduled controlled substance boxes

question: Slide 17 itemizes four key legal and ethical considerations in dementia and safeguarding. Which consideration is listed second?
options: Informed consent and treatment decisions
Lasting Power of Attorney
Privacy and confidentiality
End of Life Care and Advance Care Planning

question: Which independent support helpline number is explicitly provided on your slide resources for contacting the Alzheimer's Society Dementia Connect service?
What helpline contact is provided across Slide 18 to directly reach the Admiral Nurse Dementia Helpline at Dementia UK?
options: 0800 678 1602 Professional Practice Evaluation • Exam Paper 4 of 20 Page 6 of 8

options: Section 5
Section 21
Section 44
Section 60

options: The local trust payroll structure
